Western Pahāṛī (Sirmaurī · Jaunsārī)

The Attestations, Read

Transcription, best-effort translation, and line-break evidence for every textual attestation in Pandey & Mandal, L2/25-224, “Proposal to encode Sirmauri in Unicode.”

Overview

What this is. Best-effort readings of every textual attestation in L2/25-224 — the two Grierson press specimens, the land-grant documents, the sāñcā manuscripts, the killa inscription, and the modern digital poem — plus a cross-document analysis of line-break behaviour. Pedagogical charts (Figs. 1, 3, 5) are omitted by design.

Epistemic status. Readings were made directly from the figure images using the letter values in the proposal (§4–5) and Grierson's charts, without access to Grierson's romanization. For the printed specimens, the known content (the Parable of the Prodigal Son) scaffolds the reading. For the handwritten manuscripts, full transcription is beyond honest reach: photographs are faint, hands are cursive, and the writing is unspaced. There I report only what is genuinely defensible — invocations, formulae, names, month-names, digits — each marked with a confidence colour, alongside structural annotation. Sirmauri maps 1:1 to Devanagari; transcriptions are Devanagari with IAST beneath.
confident reading probable [guess / gap]

The corpus at a glance

TabSourceRegimeReading yield
Sirmaurī specimenFig. 2 · Grierson 1916b: 468, Dhārṭhī dialectword-spaced, ragged rightfull 12-line transcription, graded
Jaunsārī specimenFig. 4 · Grierson 1916b: 401continuous, flush rightopening formula only
Land grantsFigs. 6–12 · Shirgul Devta temple, 1770s–1890scontinuous, rulednames, a date, land vocabulary
Sāñcā booksFigs. 13–17 · Negi & Pabuch manuscriptscontinuous, ruled, rubricatedone anchored phrase, structure
Killa inscriptionFig. 18 · stone, whitewashunruleda year-word and numerals
Digital poemFig. 40 · Sherjung Chauhan, typedword-spaced versecomplete text; translation attempted

Several proposal figures photograph the same manuscript openings (e.g. Fig. 17's lower photograph = Fig. 14's upper; Figs. 19/26/27/28 re-crop pages shown elsewhere). Tabs group by document, not by figure number.

Sirmaurī specimen

Fig. 2; Grierson 1916b: 468, Dhārṭhī dialect. Hover any word to follow it across script, romanization, and gloss.

Line 01
एकीekī one जाणेjāṇe person-OBL रेre of दुdu two बेटेbeṭe sons थीएthīe were ॥  

Lk 15:11  “A certain man had two sons.”

Line 02
[काँचे]kā̃ce? younger? बेटेbeṭe son आपणेāpṇe his-own बापूbāpū father खेkhe to (DAT) बोलूbolū said ॥  

Lk 15:12  “The younger son said to his father —”

काँचे 'younger' read on the glyph run and the Nepali/Pahari cognate kāncho 'youngest'; खे is the Western Pahari dative postposition; if the first word is instead choṭe the sense is unchanged.

Line 03
बापूbāpū father! मेरेmere my बांडेbāṇḍe share-OBL राrā of धनdhan wealth [मूँ खे]mū̃ khe? to me? देde give! ॥  

Lk 15:12  ““Father, give me the wealth of my share.””

बांड 'share, division' (cf. Hindi bā̃ṭ) recurs three times in the specimen and anchors this whole section.

Line 04
[तिणी]tiṇī? he-ERG? [?]… ? आपणाāpṇā his-own धनdhan wealth बांडीbāṇḍī having-divided देआdeā gave ॥  

Lk 15:12  “He divided the wealth and gave (it to them).”

One glyph run after the first word resisted reading — plausibly 'between the two of them'.

Line 05
थोड़ेthoṛe few दिनाँdinā̃ days पाछेpāche after काँचेkā̃ce younger बेटेbeṭe son आपणाāpṇā his-own 

Lk 15:13  “A few days after, the younger son … his”

No daṇḍā: the sentence flows over the line boundary (at a word boundary).

Line 06
बांडाbāṇḍā share [?]… gathered? [?]… ? दूरdūr far देशाdeśā country-OBL खेkhe to 

Lk 15:13  “… share (gathered up, and) toward a far country”

Line 07
चली गआcalī gaā went-away ॥  [तेती]tetī? there? आपणाāpṇā his-own मालmāl goods मोजमँगा–mojamã̄gā– riotous-… 

Lk 15:13  “went away. There his goods in riotous- —”

The line runs past every other line's margin and the final word appears to continue onto line 8 — see the Line breaks tab, Exhibit A.

Line 08
–ड़ा–ṛā …-living-of उड़ाईuḍāī squandered देआdeā away ॥  [जाले]jāle? when? साराsārā all मालmāl goods 

Lk 15:13–14  “—living squandered away. When all the goods”

Line 09
[मुकी]mukī? finished? गआgaā went (AUX) [ताले]tāle? then? तिसtis that-OBL देशाdeśā country-OBL माँmā̃ in [बड़ा]baṛā? great? कालkāl famine 

Lk 15:14  “ran out, then in that country a great famine”

मुकी read via Punjabi/Pahari mukṇā 'to be finished'; काल 'famine' fits both glyphs and the parable.

Line 10
पड़ाpaṛā fell ॥  [तेबे]tebe? then? सेse he कंगालkaṅgāl destitute [होणे लागा]hoṇe lāgā? began to be? ॥  

Lk 15:14  “fell. Then he began to be destitute.”

Line 11
[तेबे]tebe? then? सेse he एकीekī one जाणेjāṇe person-OBL [रे पाखे]re pākhe? to the side of? [गआ]gaā? went? 

Lk 15:15  “Then he went (and attached himself) to a certain man …”

The re-occurrence of एकी जाणे from line 1 is clearly legible mid-line and anchors this verse.

Line 12
[?]राखेआ…rākheā? kept? ॥  [तिणी]tiṇī? he-ERG? सेse him [सूअराँ]sūarā̃? swine? चारणेcārṇe to-graze [खे देआ]khe deā? sent? 

Lk 15:15  “… (who) kept him. He sent him to graze (swine) …”

Reading confidence drops here; the figure ends before the sentence completes.

The passage as a whole

15:11 A certain man had two sons. 12 The younger son said to his father, “Father, give me the wealth of my share.” And he divided the property between them. 13 A few days after, the younger son gathered up his share and went away to a far country, and there squandered his goods in riotous living. 14 When all the goods were spent, a great famine fell upon that country, and he began to be destitute. 15 Then he went to a certain man (of that country, who) kept him, and sent him to graze (swine)… — the figure reproduces only the first page of the specimen; the text breaks off here.

Jaunsārī specimen

Fig. 4; Grierson 1916b: 401. Standard dialect, District Dehra Dun. The full thirteen lines, read against the parable. Segmentation is conjectural throughout — the hand writes without word spaces.

How to read this tab — and how much to trust it. This hand is cursive, dense, and unspaced: honest per-akshara accuracy is limited. Of the roughly 360 aksharas on the page I would defend perhaps a sixth as read (dark ink / blue), another portion as probable, and the rest — everything marked ° and bracketed — as reconstruction: the Parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:11–20) aligned clause-by-clause to the lines, accepted only where visible glyph shapes are compatible. Chunk divisions with ° are my conjectured word joints, not marks on the page. Treat every bracketed form as a hypothesis; the check is Grierson’s romanization, LSI IX.iv pp. 402–403, which I could not access. The verse map (which line carries which verse) is more robust than any individual word: it is pinned by four anchors — the vocative बाबा opening line 2, the bāb re-entry at line 9, आपणा बाब opening line 11, and a majūrā̃-cluster in line 12.
Line 01 · Lk 15:11–12a
[कोई° एक°]koī ek? a certain [जणा° के°]jaṇā ke? man-GEN दुइdui two बेटाbeṭā sons [थिये°]thiye? were [तिनआं° मां° का°]tinā̃ mā̃ kā? of them [कांसा°]kā̃sā? younger 

“A certain man had two sons; of them the younger …”

The opening formula is fixed by the parable; दुइ and बेटा shapes resolve directly. Whether the line ends inside the next word or at its boundary is unresolvable — the first of twelve such boundaries.

Line 02 · Lk 15:12
बाबाbābā father! जोjo which [मेरा°]merā? my [बांटो°]bā̃ṭo? share [आवे°]āve? comes [सो°]so? that [मूं° के°]mū̃ ke? to me [दे°]de? give! […]…  बाब°bāb? father 

““Father, that which comes as my share — give (it) me.” …”

The strongest anchor on the page: the line opens with the doubled ba-shapes of बाबा in vocative position, exactly where the son's plea begins. A second bāb-run later in the line fits the narrator resuming (“…the father…”).

Line 03 · Lk 15:12b–13a
[जायदाद°]jāydād? property [बांटी°]bā̃ṭī? divided [दिनी°]dinī? gave [॥?]  [थोड़ा°]thoṛā? few [दिनां°]dinā̃? days [पीछे°]pīchhe? after 

“… he divided the property (between them). A few days after —”

A run consistent with jāydād 'property' (with its doubled da) sits mid-line; the division clause and the 'few days after' opener share this line if the verse map holds.

Line 04 · Lk 15:13
कांसा°kā̃sā? younger बेटा°beṭā? son [सब° कुछ°]sab kuch? everything [बटोली° के°]baṭolī ke? having gathered [दूर°]dūr? far [देसा° के°]desā ke? country-to [चली° ग–°]calī ga–? went a- 

“the younger son gathered everything up and to a far country went a—”

Candidate mid-word carryover: the line's final akshara-cluster reads like the verb's first syllable, with the vowel completing on line 5 — the second candidate split in the corpus (after Fig. 2's Exhibit A).

Line 05 · Lk 15:13b
[–ो°]–o? -way [अर°]ar? and [तेती°]tetī? there [आपणो°]āpṇo? his-own [माल°]māl? goods [लुच्चपणे° मां°]luccpaṇe mā̃? in riotous-living [उड़ाई°]uḍāī? squandered [दिनो°]dino? away 

“—way, and there squandered his goods in riotous living.”

Possessive āpṇ- shapes are identifiable; the 'riotous living' phrase is supplied by the parable and Fig. 2's parallel, not by secure glyph reading.

Line 06 · Lk 15:14a
[जब°]jab? when [सब°]sab? all [खर्ची° चुको°]kharcī cuko? was spent [तेस°]tes? that [देसा° मां°]desā mā̃? country-in [बड़ो°]baṛo? great काळ°kāḷ? famine [पड़ो°]paṛo? fell 

“When all was spent, a great famine fell on that country,”

A kāl/kāḷ-compatible cluster sits in the right half; the line opens with a possessive-looking run I could not firmly place — the verse map, not the glyphs, does the work here.

Line 07 · Lk 15:14b–15a
[अर° से°]ar se? and he [कंगाल°]kaṅgāl? destitute [होण° लागो°]hoṇ lāgo? began to be [तेबे° से°]tebe se? then he [तेस° देसा° का°]tes desā kā? of that country [एक° जणा° के°]ek jaṇā ke? to a man [जाई° मिळो°]jāī miḷo? went and joined 

“and he began to be destitute. Then he went and joined himself to a man of that country;”

Line 08 · Lk 15:15b–16
[तिण° से°]tiṇ se? he him [आपणा°]āpṇā? his-own [खेतां° मां°]khetā̃ mā̃? fields-in [सूअर°]sūar? swine [चारणा° के°]cārṇā ke? to graze [भेजो°]bhejo? sent […]… (v16 begins?) 

“he sent him into his fields to graze swine. (And he longed to fill his belly with the husks the swine ate …)”

A doubled ba-run appears where none is expected if this line is pure v15 — either v16's vocabulary begins here or my verse map is one clause loose. Flagged rather than forced.

Line 09 · Lk 15:17
[मेरे°]mere? my बाब°bāb? father [का°]kā? of [केतरा°]ketrā? how-many [मजूरां° के°]majūrā̃ ke? hired-men-to रोटी°roṭī? bread [बचे°]bace? to spare [अर° हाउं°]ar hāũ? and I [भूखा°]bhūkhā? hungry 

““How many of my father's hired men have bread to spare, and I (perish) hungry!””

The bāb-run recurring here is what pins vv. 16–17 to lines 8–9: the father re-enters the story precisely at v17, and the shapes oblige.

Line 10 · Lk 15:17b–18a
[एती°]etī? here [भूखा°]bhūkhā? hungry [मरूं°]marū̃? I-die [॥?]  [हाउं°]hāũ? I [उठी° के°]uṭhī ke? having-risen [?]? (पास? बाब?) 

““… I die of hunger here. I will arise —””

The line's tail carries a doubled labial run readable as either पास 'to (his) presence' or a bāb-form; either way the arising-and-going clause straddles into line 11. Order of the candidates cannot be fixed at this image quality.

Line 11 · Lk 15:18
आपणा°āpṇā? my-own बाब°bāb? father [के° पास°]ke pās? to the presence of [जाउलो°]jāulo? I-will-go [अर° बोलुलो°]ar bolulo? and will-say [बाबा°]bābā? father! [असमाना° रा°]asmānā rā? of heaven [तेरा° अगाड़ी°]terā agāṛī? before thee [पाप° कीयो°]pāp kīyo? sinned 

““— and go to my father, and will say: Father, I have sinned against heaven and before thee;””

आपणा बाब opens the line in clean letters — the page's second-best anchor after line 2's बाबा.

Line 12 · Lk 15:19–20a
[तेरो° बेटो°]tero beṭo? thy son [बाजणा° जोगो°]bājṇā jogo? worthy to-be-called [नी° रयो°]nī rayo? no-more am [मूं° के°]mū̃ ke? me मजूरां°majūrā̃? hired-men [मां° रा° एक° जसो°]mā̃ rā ek jaso? as one of [बणाई° ले°]baṇāī le? make! 

““I am no more worthy to be called thy son: make me as one of thy hired men.””

A majūrā̃-compatible cluster (the parable's 'hired servants', echoing line 9) sits mid-line and anchors v19.

Line 13 · Lk 15:20a
[तेबे° से°]tebe se? then he [उठी° के°]uṭhī ke? arose and [आपणा°]āpṇā? his-own [बाब° के°]bāb ke? father-to [पास°]pās? presence [आयो°]āyo? came 

“Then he arose and came to his father …”

The page's one short line — the specimen continues on Grierson's next page, not reproduced in the proposal.

The passage as a whole

15:11 A certain man had two sons. 12 The younger said to his father, “Father, give me the share that falls to me” — and he divided his property between them. 13 A few days after, the younger son gathered everything up, went away to a far country, and there squandered his goods in riotous living. 14 When all was spent, a great famine fell on that country, and he began to be destitute. 15 He went and joined himself to a man of that country, who sent him into his fields to graze swine. 16 (He longed to fill his belly with the husks the swine ate…) 17 “How many of my father’s hired men have bread to spare, and I die here of hunger! 18 I will arise and go to my father and say: Father, I have sinned against heaven and before thee; 19 I am no more worthy to be called thy son — make me as one of thy hired men.” 20 Then he arose and came to his father … — where the reproduced page ends.

Line-break notes from this reading (cross-referenced on the Line breaks tab): line 4 ends in what reads as the first syllable of the final verb with its vowel completing on line 5 — a second candidate mid-word carryover; lines 3, 7, 10 and 12 end mid-clause with no punctuation; the only short line is the last. Twelve internal line boundaries in unspaced running prose make some mid-word breaks statistically near-certain even where no individual case can be certified at this image quality.

Land grants

Figs. 6–12 · Records of land donation to the Shirgul Devta temple, spanning six rājās of Sirmaur, 1770s–1890s. Continuous scribal hands on ruled paper, under circular seals. These are muāfī-type revenue documents.

Fig. 6 (right) — rājā Jagat Prakash (1856–1898)

The right-hand document, enlarged:

Readable fragments (positions: header line, then body lines 2–3):

[महाराजा…?]mahārājā…? header formula? गोपालgopāl Gopāl (name) [देहा?]dehā? ? [कृष्णा?]kr̥ṣṇā? Kr̥ṣṇā (name)? देवेdeve god-OBL खोkho to (DAT) गोपालgopāl Gopāl again [गाव?]gāv? village? 

गोपाल appears twice (lines 2 and 3) in clean letters — almost certainly the grantee, plausibly a temple priest; खो is the dative postposition (the specimen's khe in a scribal variant); the header line under the seal is the ruler's protocol, resisting confident reading beyond its shape. The left-hand document (Shamsher Prakash, 1770–89) is the oldest item in the corpus and too faint for word-level reading.

Fig. 8 — the most legible grant: line by line

The upper block, in strips (read what you can — the notes below record what I would defend):

Header protocol: a Sanskritized deed-title; best guess कागीद° पानो° [??] …पत्रम्° — kāgīd 'document' + pāno 'leaf/paper' with a -patram-like tail. Guess grade throughout.

[पक्को°]pakko? confirmed/permanent [सात°]sāt? seven? गाँउgā̃u village(s) — — — (blank) [जगा°]jagā? land [जमा°]jamā? assessment [खेते°]khete? fields जोतकार°jotkār? cultivator 

The body speaks pure revenue register: pakko 'confirmed' is exactly what a permanent (pakkā) muāfī grant would say; गाँउ recurs; jot- 'cultivation' holds up at this resolution. The run of dashes now reads more plausibly as a deliberate blank — space left where names or details were to be filled — than as margin padding; I have corrected the Line breaks tab accordingly. An interlinear insertion rides above the following line: the scribe's correction convention.

१८६[०°]186[0?] VS year वे°ve? in-the साल°sāl? year […]… month + day 

The date line, revised. My earlier pass read the second word as vaiśākh; at 3× the shapes resolve better as वे साल 'in the year' — so: “in the year (VS) 186[0?] …” with month and day following in effaced letters. The chronology now has a checkable wrinkle: VS 1860 = 1803 CE, which falls at the accession of Karma Prakash (1804–15), not within Fateh Prakash's reign (1815–50) as the figure caption has it. Either the fourth digit is ९ rather than ६ (VS 189x = 1833–43 CE, fitting the caption), or the caption's attribution merits a second look — a small, falsifiable flag for the proposal authors.

The lower half of the sheet is a second block written rotated 90° to the first: sheet orientation was a free variable.

Figs. 9–11 — rājā Raghbir Prakash II (1850–56)

Three grants of a six-year reign, evidently a single chancery practice: body text in a rapid cursive hand between rules, with a vertical column of text running up the left margin (clearest in Figs. 9 and 11) — marginal attestation or docketing at right angles to the body, a layout convention these documents share with wider North-Indian chancery practice. Fig. 11's final line carries digit shapes (a date or sum); individual digits did not resolve at this image quality. Long flourished strokes cross the page below the seals — signature paraphs. Word-level reading of the cursive body was not achievable; the hands abbreviate and join letters beyond what the available photographs support.

Fig. 12 — rājā Shamsher Prakash (1856–98)

Body lines 2–5, enlarged:

The steadiest scribal hand among the grants — and the page the proposal itself mines for the variant ha (its Fig. 32 highlights come from this document family). An interlinear insertion is visible above the fourth body line (small letters riding the gap). The writing is continuous; the only whitespace on the page is structural (around the seal and the paraph), not lexical. Word-level yield here was limited to shapes consistent with the same revenue register as Fig. 8 ([deve? 'to the god', [māph? 'remitted'] — both guesses).

Fig. 7 — rājā Karma Prakash (1804–1815)

Two sealed blocks on one sheet — grant and (plausibly) confirmation, each under its own seal with a centred protocol line. Too faint for word-level reading; included for completeness and for its layout: the double-block structure recurs in Fig. 6.

Sāñcā books

Figs. 13–17 · sāñcā grantha: ritual, calendrical and astrological handbooks — the script's living habitat. This pass exploits what manuscripts give that prose doesn't: closed-class lists, serial numbers, repeated formulae, and arithmetic that certifies its own reading.

Fig. 15 — a table that proves its own digits

The paired digit columns resolve, row by row, to

3·13 4·12 5·11 6·10 7·9 8·8
9·7 10·6 11·5 12·4 13·3 14·2 15·1

— thirteen rows of (n, 16−n) for n = 3…15. The constant sum is the point: it disambiguates every marginal १-vs-२ curl (my first pass misread two cells; the arithmetic corrected them), so the digit identifications are mutually certified — the strongest digit attestation in the corpus, and independent confirmation of the proposed digit repertoire. The proposal's own Fig. 25 crops this same table as its digit example.

The keyed column beside the numbers is a closed-class list: thirteen entries, each a short variable head + an identical two-akshara tail (a classifier — candidates -vār 'day' or a unit word; unresolved). What in a sāñcā pairs a key with (n, 16−n)? Sixteen is the number of the moon's kalās; a tithi-keyed table of waxing/waning portions is the natural interpretation — flagged as plausible, not proven. The individual heads resisted certain reading; the structure did not.

Fig. 24 — the serial column

A boxed column of serials reading cleanly १ २ ३ ४ ५ ६ ७ ८ ९ १० ११ (१२) — every digit form matching the proposed repertoire, in a single scribal hand, in sequence. Beside each serial, an entry of 2–5 aksharas (some carrying an internal numeral — these are data rows, not just names). Twelve serially-numbered entries in an astrological handbook invites the rāśi list; the first entry's shapes are compatible with मकर° makara, which would make it a Capricorn-first (Māgh-first) cycle — one compatible guess, the rest left unforced.

Fig. 17 (upper) — the recurring formula, compared across its instances

Four sections' opening lines, stacked. The method: a formula that repeats survives a rough hand better than any single instance — divergences between instances mark the variable slot. What the comparison shows: (1) a fixed opener of ~5 aksharas whose core cluster is compatible with परष°/बरष° varṣa 'year'; (2) a variable middle; (3) a shared tail. With the incrementing section numbers, the natural reading of the page is a set of year-indexed prognostic entries — varṣaphala-type: “[in the Nth year] … [outcome]” — flagged as an interpretation, with the repetition itself as the hard datum.

The boxed rubrics are near-identical with a varying middle element, followed by the section number. Exact digit values resisted certainty at this image quality, but the increment-by-one pattern across the page is visually secure. Continuation lines hang deeply indented under their section's first line; a double rule frames the page.

Fig. 14 (upper) = Fig. 17 (lower) — the prose page as a workbench

The boldest manuscript hand in the corpus, in strips. I read fragments, not sentences — the strips are embedded at working resolution so the readings can be checked and extended.

Lines 1–2, diplomatic attempt (° = uncertain akshara, ·?· = unread): [?]ज°न°शा°/क्षा° पापा° गा°ळा° ⁚ ·?· … and in line 2 a mid-line double daṇḍā followed by …गागा°-like reduplication. Three concrete observations survive: the two-dot mark ⁚ falls mid-line (the proposal's Fig. 29 punctuation category, in situ); reduplicated syllable runs (पापा-, गागा-like) recur — formulaic or onomastic; and the daṇḍā positions I see match the proposal's own Fig. 28 annotations of this page, a useful sanity-check on my line registration.

Line 4 ends in shapes compatible with आई° गआ° 'came' — if right, the clause completes exactly at the margin; if the last akshara belongs to line 5's opener, it is another unmarked carryover. Undecidable at this quality; both readings are line-break-relevant.

The final line is short and centred — the page's only ragged line, closing the entry. The companion page (Fig. 14 lower) is tabular: ruled columns of digit + name entries, the working tables this prose serves.

Fig. 13 — Natiram Negi's Jaunsari sāñcā

The Jaunsari branch of the tradition. The structural contrast with Fig. 17: boxed rubrics fall inline — mid-line and at line-end — with running text flowing around them, rather than anchoring the left margin. Recurring short tokens (plausibly the prognostic bhalā ho- 'it will be well' family — guess) repeat across entries. Writing continuous; lines filled to the ruled edge; letters visibly compressed approaching the margin.

One anchored phrase

This manuscript family carries the corpus's one externally confirmed phrase — the proposal's Fig. 37 reads, on a diagram page of the same book:

श्रीsarī [śrī] holy गणेसgaṇesa Gaṇeśa आयāya -to नमःnamaḥ obeisance 

— śrī gaṇeśāya namaḥ, spelled sarī gaṇesa-āya namaḥ: sa standing in for śa, the Sanskrit rendered as heard, with the visarga in its only attested outing.

Killa inscription

Fig. 18 · whitewash on the stone of a killa (tower) beside a house — the corpus's one epigraph.

Three lines, unruled, on coursed masonry. Line 1 is too effaced for any reading. Line 2 opens with a run plausibly reading साल sāl 'year' followed by digit shapes; line 3 carries at least two further numeral groups. The genre almost writes itself — a construction record: year, and figures (dimensions, cost, or date-parts).

[साल?]sāl? year? [१९…?]19…? digits [६१?]61? numerals […]… effaced 

Line-break-wise the inscription is its own regime: each line is laid out on its own stone course, so line units may simply be layout units. No word spacing is evident.

Digital poem

Fig. 40 · A poem by Sherjung Chauhan, typed in Sirmauri and Devanagari with digital fonts — the script's present tense. Transcription is certain (the Devanagari parallel is the author's own); the translation is my best effort, bracketed where the Sirmauri lexicon defeats me.

What the poem is about. The place-name पझोते (Pajhota) in couplet 2, with बगावती 'rebellion', हुक्मों जारी 'orders issued', and जोयल 'jail' following, reads as a song of the Pajhota movement — the 1942 peasant agitation in Sirmaur state — sung to one Sūrat Singh: men leave home and cattle for the cause, the king's orders send them to jail by turns, and the women are left to speak of them as already belonging to a foreign land. Word-level renderings below are graded; the theme is not in doubt.

बोयदा बोलू सूरतसिंगा, तेरी लाणी ओ बातो रे,

Boydā says: O Sūrat Singh — your [लाणी: telling-worthy?] words, O!

जादी री तांईं हांडदा फिरा, देस देखो न रातो रे॥

For the sake of [जादी: property? a name?] he wanders roaming; he sees not (his) country, nor its nights.

रोहणा बे छोड़ा, सोहणा बे छोड़ा, छोड़ा तिणिए ठाठो रे,

Rohana he left, Sohana he left; he left (all) comfort behind,

पझोते रे नोइए मांछे बे देता, तेसरा पूरा साथो रे॥

to the new [मांछे: cause? men?] of Pajhota he gave his full support.

राजे तांईं पाँची इनो मांछो री, बगावती री बातो रे,

To the king reached (word) of these [मांछो] — talk of rebellion —

तिणिए बे की इन्दरी बोलो, बोंद सारी बाटो रे॥

[तिणिए बे की इन्दरी बोलो?] … all the [बोंद: words? drops?] along the way.

बुका न दोबा तोबे बे राजा रे, किया हुक्मों जारी रे,

[बुका न दोबा?] then the king, O — he issued his orders:

होसयो डोए खेलेयो डोए, जोयल के बारी-बारी रे॥

[होसयो डोए खेलेयो डोए?] — to jail, turn by turn.

छेड़ू बे छोड़ी छोटू बे छोड़ी, घोरो छोड़ी तिने बारो रे,

They left the [छेड़ू: cattle?], left the little ones, left their homes, those [बारो: twelve? banished?],

उम्बरे भोरे खे जोयलो लोए, माने न तिनें बोलो हारो रे॥

at [उम्बरे भोरे: break of dawn?] taken to jail — they would not concede their word defeated.

सनानी थेए से जादी रे बोलो, ओओ तिंदरा भोला रे,

The womenfolk then spoke of [जादी]: 'O, their innocent ones —'

चिते नी राखो जू आज तिनों, बोयरे देशो रा होला रे॥

'keep them not in mind: today they are [होला?] of a foreign land.'

Refrain: the first couplet repeats at the end. Note for the encoding file: this is exactly the modern regime — space-separated words, phrase-final daṇḍās, breaks at verse boundaries — that the proposal's LineBreak.txt models.

Line breaks

Does Sirmauri break within words? The attested answer: it depends on who is writing — and the two regimes matter differently for encoding.

Fig. 2 · Sirmaurī specimen

right-edge ink extent per line · spread ≈ 35% of measure
ragged right — breaks chosen at word boundaries→

Fig. 4 · Jaunsārī specimen

right-edge ink extent per line · spread ≈ 4% of measure
flush right — line ends wherever space runs out→

Bars show the horizontal ink extent of each text line (automatic measurement on the figure scans; a few adjacent lines merged in detection and were hand-corrected, so treat the numbers as approximate — the qualitative contrast is robust and visible to the naked eye). The manuscript pages pattern with Fig. 4.

Exhibit A — a probable intra-word break in the word-spaced specimen. End of line 7 (top) and start of line 8 (bottom), Fig. 2. Line 7 is the most over-extended line of the page (the scribe squeezing), and line 8 opens with a short bound-morpheme-looking syllable followed by a larger-than-usual gap. Reading: मोजमँगा- | ड़ा — one word carried over the line boundary with no hyphen or continuation mark. Confidence: moderate.
Exhibit B — the right margin of a sāñcā manuscript (Fig. 17, lower photograph = Fig. 14, upper). Lines run to the ruled edge; letters compress or ride up at the boundary; no hyphenation, filler, or continuation marks. The land grants (Figs. 6–12) behave the same way — with one refinement: Fig. 8's run of dashes — on closer reading more plausibly a deliberate blank left for names than margin padding — shows the scribe treating line space as a form to be filled either way.

Findings

Two regimes coexist. Grierson's edited Sirmauri specimen — and modern digital text (Fig. 40's poem) — is word-spaced, ragged-right, breaking at word boundaries. The Jaunsari specimen and the primary manuscripts (land grants, sāñcās) are effectively scriptio continua: no systematic word spacing, lines filled flush to the ruled edge, daṇḍās falling mid-line as freely as at line end. In the continuous regime the question "does it break within words?" answers itself: with no word boundaries marked, the line boundary falls wherever the paper runs out, words included.

Intra-word breaks are unmarked but akshara-respecting. No hyphenation or continuation convention appears anywhere in the corpus. In every line-end examined, dependent signs stay attached to their base consonants: splits fall between orthographic syllables, never inside one. Even "break anywhere" is really "break at any grapheme-cluster boundary" — the standard Brahmic manuscript pattern.

Even the word-spaced regime tolerates splitting. Exhibit A suggests Grierson's scribe, running out of room, carried a word over the line without ceremony — word-boundary breaking as presentational preference, not orthographic rule.

Line structure is independent of sentence structure. Across the sāñcās, single/double daṇḍās and the dotted marks fall mid-line; across the specimens, sentences flow over line ends without punctuation. Lines are units of paper, not of sense — except in the inscription (one course, one line) and the poem (one verse, one line), where the medium supplies the unit.

Implication for the proposal's LineBreak.txt. The proposed properties (letters = AL, signs = CM, digits = NU) model the modern, spaced convention — breaks at spaces — and Fig. 40 shows this is how current users actually type. The assignment is defensible. Faithful digitization of the historical documents would represent line ends with hard breaks, as facsimile transcription always does, so the manuscripts' break-anywhere behaviour creates no pressure toward SA-style line breaking. The one reviewable nuance: since unspaced text is the majority of the historical corpus, a sentence in the proposal acknowledging that (and that AL+spaces serves the contemporary convention) would preempt the question in UTC review.

Prepared from L2/25-224 (Pandey & Mandal, 2025-10-15); specimen images from Grierson, Linguistic Survey of India IX.iv (1916), pp. 401 & 468; manuscript photographs from the proposal's figures (Shirgul Devta temple records; sāñcās of Natiram Negi and Gangaram Pabuch); poem by Sherjung Chauhan. Readings are original best-effort work, calibrated against known or expected content, unchecked against Grierson's romanization — treat every bracketed form as falsifiable. Right-edge measurements: ink-extent analysis of the figure scans.
